11 After Abraham died, God blessed his son Isaac. Isaac lived near Beer-lahai-roi.
12 This is the story of Ishmael, Abraham's son. His mother was Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah's servant.
13 These are the names of Ishmael's sons, in the order they were born. Nebaioth was the firstborn. Then came Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam,
14 Mishma, Dumah, Massa,
15 Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah.
16 These were Ishmael's twelve sons. Each one had his own village and camp. They became twelve tribal rulers.
17 Ishmael lived 137 years in all. Then he died and was gathered to his people.
18 His family settled in the land from Havilah to Shur, near the border of Egypt, on the way to Asshur. They lived in conflict with all their brothers.
19 This is the story of Isaac, Abraham's son. Abraham was the father of Isaac.
20 Isaac was forty years old when he married Rebekah. She was the daughter of Bethuel the Aramean from Paddan-aram. She was also the sister of Laban the Aramean.
21 Rebekah could not have children. So Isaac prayed to the LORD for his wife. The LORD heard his prayer, and Rebekah became pregnant.
22 But the babies pushed against each other inside her. She said, "Why is this happening to me?" So she went to ask the LORD.
23 The LORD said to her: "Two nations are inside you. Two peoples will come from you and be divided. One people will be stronger than the other. And the older will serve the younger."
24 When the time came for her to give birth, there were twins inside her.
25 The first baby came out red. His whole body was covered with hair, like a coat. So they named him Esau ("hairy").
26 Then his brother came out, holding on to Esau's heel. So they named him Jacob ("he grabs the heel"). Isaac was sixty years old when the twins were born.
27 The boys grew up. Esau became a skilled hunter who loved the open country. But Jacob was a quiet man who stayed among the tents.
28 Isaac loved to eat wild meat, so he loved Esau. But Rebekah loved Jacob.
29 One day Jacob was cooking some stew. Esau came in from the open country, very hungry.
30 He said to Jacob, "Quick! Let me eat some of that red stew. I am very hungry!" (That is why he was also called Edom, which means "red.")
31 Jacob replied, "First sell me your birthright."
32 Esau said, "Look, I am about to die. What good is a birthright to me?"
33 Jacob said, "Promise me first with an oath." So Esau gave his oath. He sold his birthright to Jacob.
34 Then Jacob gave Esau some bread and lentil soup. Esau ate and drank, got up, and went away. So Esau despised his birthright — he treated it as worthless.

title: Genesis 26
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 There was another famine in the land. It came after the famine in Abraham's time. So Isaac went to Abimelech, king of the Philistines, at Gerar.
2 The LORD appeared to Isaac and said, "Do not go down to Egypt. Live in the land where I tell you to live.
3 Stay in this land as a foreigner. I will be with you and bless you. I will give all these lands to you and your descendants. I will keep the promise I made to your father Abraham.
4 I will make your descendants as many as the stars in the sky. I will give them all these lands. Through your offspring all nations on earth will be blessed.
5 This is because Abraham obeyed me. He kept my duty, my commands, my rules, and my laws."
6 So Isaac settled in Gerar.
7 The men of that place asked about his wife. He said, "She is my sister." He was afraid to say, "She is my wife." He thought, "The men here will kill me because of Rebekah. She is very beautiful."
8 Isaac had been there a long time. One day Abimelech, king of the Philistines, looked down from his window. He was surprised to see Isaac holding his wife Rebekah close.
9 Abimelech called for Isaac and said, "She is really your wife! Why did you say, 'She is my sister'?" Isaac replied, "I thought I might die because of her."
10 "What have you done to us?" said Abimelech. "One of the men could have slept with your wife. You would have brought guilt on us."
11 So Abimelech warned all the people. He said, "Anyone who harms this man or his wife will surely die."
12 Isaac planted seed in that land. That same year he reaped a hundred times as much. The LORD blessed him.
13 He became richer and richer. In the end he was very wealthy.
14 He owned many flocks, many herds, and many servants. So the Philistines envied him.
15 The Philistines took dirt and filled all the wells. Abraham's servants had dug those wells long ago.
16 Then Abimelech said to Isaac, "Go away from us. You are much too powerful for us."
17 So Isaac left that place. He camped in the Valley of Gerar and settled there.
18 Isaac reopened the wells from his father Abraham's days. The Philistines had filled them after Abraham died. Isaac gave them the same names his father had given them.
19 Isaac's servants dug in the valley. They found a well of fresh water there.
20 But the herdsmen of Gerar fought with Isaac's herdsmen. They said, "The water is ours!" So Isaac named the well Esek, which means "Dispute," because they fought with him.
21 Then they dug another well. They fought over that one too. So he named it Sitnah, which means "Fight."

22 He moved on from there and dug another well. They did not fight over this one. He named it Rehoboth, which means "Wide Places." He said, "Now the LORD has given us room. We will be fruitful in the land."
23 From there Isaac went up to Beersheba.

24 That night the LORD appeared to him. He said, "I am the God of your father Abraham. Do not be afraid, for I am with you. I will bless you. I will increase the number of your descendants because of my servant Abraham."
25 So Isaac built an altar there and called on the name of the LORD. He set up his tent there. His servants also dug a well there.
26 Later, Abimelech came to Isaac from Gerar. He brought Ahuzzath his adviser and Phicol the commander of his army.
27 Isaac asked them, "Why have you come to me? You hated me and sent me away."
28 They replied, "We can clearly see that the LORD has been with you. Let there be an oath between us. Let us make a covenant with you.
29 Promise that you will not harm us. We did not harm you. We did only good to you. We sent you on your way in peace. And now the LORD has blessed you."
30 So Isaac prepared a feast for them. They ate and drank.
31 Early the next morning they swore an oath to each other. Then Isaac sent them on their way. They left him in peace.
32 That same day, Isaac's servants came and told him about the well they had dug. They said, "We have found water!"
33 So he called it Shibah. To this day the name of the city is Beersheba, which means "Well of the Oath."
34 When Esau was forty years old, he married two Hittite women. They were Judith daughter of Beeri the Hittite, and Basemath daughter of Elon the Hittite.
35 They were a source of grief to Isaac and Rebekah.

title: Genesis 27
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 When Isaac was old, his eyes were so weak he could not see anymore. He called his older son Esau and said, "My son." "Here I am," Esau replied.
2 "Look," said Isaac, "I am now old. I do not know the day of my death.
3 Take your weapons — your arrows and bow. Go out into the field and hunt some wild game for me.
4 Then prepare the kind of tasty food I love. Bring it to me to eat, so that I may give you my blessing before I die."
5 Now Rebekah was listening to what Isaac told his son Esau. So Esau went into the field to hunt wild game and bring it back.
6 Rebekah said to her son Jacob, "Look, I overheard your father say to your brother Esau,
7 'Bring me some wild game. Prepare a tasty dish for me to eat. Then I will bless you before the LORD before I die.'
8 Now, my son, listen to my voice. Do exactly what I tell you.
9 Go out to the flock. Bring me two good young goats. I will make them into the kind of tasty food your father loves.
10 Then take it to your father to eat, so that he may bless you before he dies."
11 Jacob answered his mother Rebekah, "Look, my brother Esau is a hairy man. But I have smooth skin.
12 What if my father touches me? Then he will know I am a liar. I will bring a curse on myself, not a blessing."
13 His mother replied, "Let your curse fall on me, my son. Just obey my voice. Go and get them for me."
14 So Jacob went and got two goats. He brought them to his mother. She made the tasty food his father loved.
15 Rebekah took the best clothes in the house. They belonged to her older son Esau. She put them on her younger son Jacob.
16 She also put the goatskins on his hands and on the smooth part of his neck.
17 Then she handed her son Jacob the tasty food and bread she had made.
18 So Jacob went to his father and said, "My father." "Here I am!" he answered. "Which one are you, my son?"
19 Jacob said to his father, "I am Esau, your firstborn. I have done as you told me. Please sit up and eat some of my game, so that you may bless me."
20 But Isaac asked his son, "How did you find it so quickly, my son?" "Because the LORD your God brought it to me," he replied.
21 Then Isaac said to Jacob, "Please come closer so I can touch you, my son. Are you really my son Esau, or not?"
22 So Jacob came close to his father Isaac. Isaac touched him and said, "The voice is the voice of Jacob, but the hands are the hands of Esau."
23 Isaac did not recognize him. His hands were hairy like the hands of his brother Esau. So Isaac blessed him.
24 Again he asked, "Are you really my son Esau?" And he replied, "I am."
25 "Serve me," said Isaac, "and let me eat some of my son's game, so that I may bless you." Jacob brought it to him, and he ate. Then he brought him wine, and he drank.
26 Then his father Isaac said to him, "Please come near and kiss me, my son."
27 So he came near and kissed him. When Isaac smelled his clothing, he blessed him and said: "Ah, the smell of my son is like the smell of a field that the LORD has blessed.
28 May God give you heaven's dew and earth's richness — plenty of grain and new wine.

29 May nations serve you and peoples bow down to you. Be lord over your brothers, and may the sons of your mother bow down to you. May those who curse you be cursed, and those who bless you be blessed."
30 As soon as Isaac had finished blessing him, Jacob left his father's presence. Then his brother Esau returned from the hunt.
31 He too made some tasty food. He brought it to his father and said, "My father, sit up and eat of your son's game, so that you may bless me."
32 But his father Isaac replied, "Who are you?" "I am Esau, your firstborn son," he answered.
33 Isaac began to shake hard. He said, "Who was it, then, who hunted the game and brought it to me? I ate it all before you came in. I blessed him — and indeed, he will be blessed!"
34 When Esau heard his father's words, he let out a loud and bitter cry. He said to his father, "Bless me — me too, my father!"
35 But Isaac replied, "Your brother came by a trick and took your blessing."

36 So Esau said, "Isn't he rightly named Jacob? This is the second time he has taken advantage of me. He took my birthright, and now he has taken my blessing!" Then he asked, "Haven't you saved a blessing for me?"

37 But Isaac answered Esau, "Look, I have made him your master. I have given him all his relatives as servants. I have fed him well with grain and new wine. What is left that I can do for you, my son?"
38 Esau said to his father, "Do you have only one blessing, my father? Bless me — me too, my father!" Then Esau wept aloud.
39 His father Isaac answered him: "Look, your dwelling will be away from earth's richness, away from the dew of heaven above.
40 You will live by the sword, and you will serve your brother. But when you grow restless, you will throw his yoke from off your neck."
41 Esau hated Jacob because of the blessing his father had given him. Esau said in his heart, "The days of mourning for my father are near. Then I will kill my brother Jacob."
42 Rebekah was told what her older son Esau had said. She sent for her younger son Jacob. She told him, "Look, your brother Esau is comforting himself by planning to kill you.
43 So now, my son, obey my voice. Run away at once to my brother Laban in Haran.
44 Stay with him for a while, until your brother's anger cools down.
45 Wait until your brother's anger against you fades. Wait until he forgets what you have done to him. Then I will send for you and bring you back. Why should I lose both of you in one day?"
46 Then Rebekah said to Isaac, "I am tired of my life because of these Hittite women. If Jacob takes a Hittite wife from among them, what good is my life?"

title: Genesis 28
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 Isaac called for Jacob and blessed him. He told him, "Do not marry a Canaanite woman.
2 Go at once to Paddan-aram, to the house of Bethuel, your mother's father. Take a wife there from the daughters of Laban, your mother's brother.
3 May God Almighty bless you. May He give you many children and increase your numbers. May you become a community of peoples.
4 May He give you the blessing given to Abraham. May He give it to you and your descendants. Then you will take and keep this land where you now live as a stranger — the land God gave to Abraham."
5 So Isaac sent Jacob away to Paddan-aram. Jacob went to Laban, the son of Bethuel from Aram. Laban was the brother of Rebekah, the mother of Jacob and Esau.
6 Esau learned that Isaac had blessed Jacob. He also learned that Isaac had sent him to Paddan-aram to find a wife. He had told Jacob, "Do not marry a Canaanite woman."
7 Esau saw that Jacob had obeyed his father and mother and gone to Paddan-aram.
8 Esau also saw that his father Isaac was not pleased with the Canaanite women.
9 So Esau went to Ishmael. He married Mahalath, the daughter of Abraham's son Ishmael. She was the sister of Nebaioth. He took her in addition to the wives he already had.
10 Meanwhile, Jacob left Beersheba and set out for Haran.
11 He came to a certain place and stopped for the night, because the sun had set. He took one of the stones there, put it under his head, and lay down to sleep.
12 Jacob had a dream. He saw a stairway resting on the earth. Its top reached up to heaven. The angels of God were going up and down on it.
13 And there at the top stood the LORD. He said, "I am the LORD, the God of your father Abraham and the God of Isaac. I will give you and your descendants the land on which you are lying.
14 Your descendants will be like the dust of the earth. You will spread out to the west and the east, the north and the south. All peoples on earth will be blessed through you and your children.
15 Look — I am with you, and I will watch over you wherever you go. I will bring you back to this land. I will not leave you until I have done what I have promised you."
16 Jacob woke up and thought, "The LORD is truly in this place, and I did not know it."
17 He was afraid and said, "This place is full of wonder! This is nothing less than the house of God. This is the gate of heaven!"
18 Early the next morning, Jacob took the stone he had put under his head. He set it up as a pillar. He poured oil on top of it.
19 He named that place Bethel. Before this, the city had been called Luz.
20 Then Jacob made a promise. He said, "If God will be with me and watch over me on this journey, and if He will give me food to eat and clothes to wear,
21 so that I return safely to my father's house, then the LORD will be my God.
22 And this stone I have set up as a pillar will be God's house. And of all that You give me, I will surely give You a tenth."

title: Genesis 29
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 Then Jacob started his journey again. He came to the land of the people of the east.
2 He looked and saw a well in the field. Three flocks of sheep lay near it. The sheep drank water from this well. A large stone covered the mouth of the well.
3 All the flocks would gather there. Then the shepherds would roll the stone away. They would water the sheep. After that, they would put the stone back over the mouth of the well.
4 "My brothers," Jacob asked the shepherds, "where are you from?" "We are from Haran," they answered.
5 "Do you know Laban, the grandson of Nahor?" Jacob asked. "We know him," they replied.
6 "Is he well?" Jacob asked. "Yes," they answered. "Look — here comes his daughter Rachel with his sheep."
7 "Look," said Jacob, "it is still the middle of the day. It is not yet time to gather the animals. Water the sheep and take them back to the field."
8 But they replied, "We cannot. We must wait until all the flocks have gathered. Then the stone will be rolled away from the mouth of the well. After that, we will water the sheep."
9 While he was still talking with them, Rachel arrived with her father's sheep. She looked after the sheep.

10 As soon as Jacob saw Rachel, he went over to the well. Rachel was the daughter of his mother's brother Laban. Jacob rolled the stone away from the mouth of the well. Then he watered his uncle's sheep.
11 Then Jacob kissed Rachel and cried out loud.
12 He told Rachel that he was the son of Rebekah, a relative of her father. So she ran and told her father.
13 When Laban heard the news about his sister's son Jacob, he ran out to meet him. He hugged him and kissed him. Then he brought him to his home. Jacob told Laban all that had happened.
14 Then Laban said, "You are indeed my own flesh and blood." So Jacob stayed with him for a month.
15 Then Laban said to him, "Just because you are my relative, should you work for nothing? Tell me what your wages should be."
16 Now Laban had two daughters. The older one was named Leah, and the younger one was named Rachel.
17 Leah had weak eyes, but Rachel had a lovely figure and was beautiful.
18 Jacob loved Rachel. So he answered, "I will serve you seven years for your younger daughter Rachel."
19 Laban replied, "Better that I give her to you than to another man. Stay here with me."
20 So Jacob served seven years to get Rachel. But they seemed like only a few days to him, because of his love for her.
21 Finally Jacob said to Laban, "Give me my wife. My time is complete, and I want to sleep with her."
22 So Laban invited all the men of that place. He made a big feast.
23 But when evening came, Laban took his daughter Leah and gave her to Jacob. And Jacob slept with her.
24 Laban also gave his servant girl Zilpah to his daughter Leah as her servant.
25 When morning came, there was Leah! "What have you done to me?" Jacob said to Laban. "Wasn't it for Rachel that I served you? Why have you tricked me?"
26 Laban replied, "It is not the way we do things here. We do not give the younger daughter in marriage before the older one.
27 Finish this wedding week. Then we will give you the younger one too, in return for another seven years of work."
28 And Jacob did just that. He finished the wedding week. Then Laban gave him his daughter Rachel as his wife.
29 Laban also gave his servant girl Bilhah to his daughter Rachel as her servant.
30 Jacob slept with Rachel too. And he loved Rachel more than Leah. So he worked for Laban another seven years.
31 When the LORD saw that Leah was not loved, he let her have children. But Rachel could not have children.
32 Leah became pregnant and gave birth to a son. She named him Reuben, which means "see, a son." She said, "The LORD has seen my misery. Surely my husband will love me now."
33 She became pregnant again and gave birth to a son. She said, "Because the LORD heard that I am not loved, he has given me this son too." So she named him Simeon, which means "one who hears."

34 Once again Leah became pregnant and gave birth to a son. She said, "Now at last my husband will become close to me, because I have given him three sons." So he was named Levi, which means "attached."
35 She became pregnant once more and gave birth to a son. She said, "This time I will praise the LORD." So she named him Judah, which means "praise." Then Leah stopped having children.

title: Genesis 30
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 Rachel saw that she had no children for Jacob. She was jealous of her sister. She said to Jacob, "Give me children, or I will die!"
2 Jacob became angry with Rachel. He said, "Am I in the place of God? He is the one who has kept you from having children."
3 Then she said, "Here is my servant Bilhah. Sleep with her. She can have children for me. In this way I too can build a family through her."
4 So Rachel gave Jacob her servant Bilhah as a wife. He slept with her.
5 Bilhah became pregnant and gave birth to a son for Jacob.
6 Then Rachel said, "God has judged in my favor. He has heard my prayer and given me a son." So she named him Dan.
7 Rachel's servant Bilhah became pregnant again. She gave birth to a second son for Jacob.
8 Then Rachel said, "I have had a hard fight with my sister, and I have won." So she named him Naphtali.
9 Leah saw that she had stopped having children. So she gave her servant Zilpah to Jacob as a wife.
10 Leah's servant Zilpah gave birth to a son for Jacob.
11 Then Leah said, "What good luck!" So she named him Gad.
12 Then Leah's servant Zilpah gave birth to a second son for Jacob.
13 Leah said, "I am so happy! The women will call me happy." So she named him Asher.
14 During the wheat harvest, Reuben went out into the field. He found some mandrake plants. He brought them to his mother Leah. Rachel said to Leah, "Please give me some of your son's mandrakes."

15 But Leah replied, "Is it not enough that you have taken my husband? Now you also want to take my son's mandrakes?" "Very well," said Rachel. "He may sleep with you tonight in exchange for your son's mandrakes."
16 That evening Jacob came in from the field. Leah went out to meet him. She said, "You must come with me. I have hired you with my son's mandrakes." So he slept with her that night.
17 God listened to Leah. She became pregnant and gave birth to a fifth son for Jacob.
18 Then Leah said, "God has paid me back. I gave my servant to my husband." So she named him Issachar.
19 Leah became pregnant again. She gave birth to a sixth son for Jacob.
20 She said, "God has given me a good gift. This time my husband will treat me with honor. I have given him six sons." So she named him Zebulun.
21 After that, Leah gave birth to a daughter. She named her Dinah.
22 Then God remembered Rachel. He listened to her and opened her womb.
23 She became pregnant and gave birth to a son. She said, "God has taken away my shame."
24 She named him Joseph and said, "May the LORD add to me another son."
25 After Rachel gave birth to Joseph, Jacob said to Laban, "Send me on my way. I want to go back to my own land.
26 Give me my wives and my children. I have worked for them. Then I will go. You know how hard I have worked for you."
27 But Laban replied, "If I have found favor in your eyes, please stay. I have learned by signs that the LORD has blessed me because of you."
28 He added, "Tell me your wages, and I will pay you."
29 Then Jacob answered, "You know how I have served you. You know how your farm animals have grown strong with my care.
30 You had very little before I came. Now your wealth has grown many times over. The LORD has blessed you wherever I have gone. But when can I also work for my own family?"
31 "What can I give you?" Laban asked. Jacob replied, "You do not need to give me anything. But do this one thing for me. Then I will keep on caring for your flocks.

32 Today let me go through all your flocks. I will take out every sheep with small spots or large spots. I will take out every dark-colored lamb. I will take out every goat with small spots or large spots. These will be my wages.
33 Later you can check my flocks. My honesty will speak for me. If I have any goats without spots, or any lambs that are not dark, they will be counted as stolen."
34 "Agreed," said Laban. "Let it be as you have said."

35 That very day Laban took out all the male goats with stripes or spots. He took out every female goat with spots — every one that had any white on it. He took out every dark-colored lamb. He gave them to his sons to care for.
36 Then he put three days' travel between himself and Jacob. Jacob cared for the rest of Laban's flocks.
37 But Jacob took fresh branches from poplar, almond, and plane trees. He peeled off the bark to show the white wood inside.
38 He set the peeled branches in the water bowls where the animals drank. So when the animals came to drink, they were ready to mate.
39 They mated in front of the branches. They gave birth to young animals with stripes, small spots, or large spots.
40 Jacob set these young animals apart. He made the rest face the dark-colored and striped sheep in Laban's flocks. So he kept his own animals apart. He did not put them with Laban's animals.
41 Whenever the stronger females were ready to mate, Jacob placed the branches in the water bowls. The animals could see them. They mated in front of the branches.
42 But if the animals were weak, he did not put out the branches. So the weak animals went to Laban. The strong animals went to Jacob.
43 In this way Jacob became very rich. He owned large flocks, women and men servants, and camels and donkeys.

title: Genesis 31
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 Now Jacob heard what Laban's sons were saying. "Jacob has taken everything our father owned. He has built all this wealth from what is ours."
2 Jacob also saw Laban's face. Laban's attitude toward him had changed.
3 Then the LORD said to Jacob, "Go back to the land of your fathers and to your relatives. I will be with you."
4 So Jacob sent for Rachel and Leah. He called them out to the field where his flocks were.
5 He said to them, "I can see from your father's face that his attitude toward me has changed. But the God of my father has been with me.
6 You know that I have served your father with all my strength.
7 Yet he has cheated me and changed my wages ten times. But God has not let him harm me.
8 If he said, 'The speckled ones will be your wages,' then the whole flock had speckled young. If he said, 'The streaked ones will be your wages,' then the whole flock had streaked young.
9 So God has taken your father's livestock away and given them to me.
10 In the breeding season, I had a dream. I saw that the male goats mating with the flock were streaked, speckled, or spotted.
11 In the dream the angel of God said to me, 'Jacob!' I replied, 'Here I am.'
12 He said, 'Look up. See that all the males mating with the flock are streaked, spotted, or speckled. For I have seen all that Laban has done to you.
13 I am the God of Bethel. There you poured oil on the pillar. There you made a serious promise to me. Now get up. Leave this land at once. Go back to your native land.'"
14 Rachel and Leah replied, "Do we have any share or inheritance left in our father's house?
15 Does he not treat us like outsiders? He has sold us. He has also wasted the money paid for us.
16 All the wealth that God has taken from our father belongs to us and our children. So do whatever God has told you."
17 Then Jacob got up. He put his children and his wives on camels.
18 He drove all his livestock before him. He took all the things he had gained in Paddan-aram. He set out for his father Isaac in the land of Canaan.
19 While Laban was away cutting the wool from his sheep, Rachel stole her father's household gods.
20 Jacob also tricked Laban the Aramean. He did not tell Laban that he was running away.
21 So Jacob fled with everything he had. He crossed the Euphrates River. He headed for the hill country of Gilead.
22 On the third day Laban was told that Jacob had fled.
23 So Laban took his relatives with him. He chased Jacob for seven days. He caught up with him in the hill country of Gilead.
24 But that night God came to Laban the Aramean in a dream. He warned him, "Be careful. Do not say anything to Jacob, either good or bad."
25 Jacob had set up his tent in the hill country of Gilead. Laban caught up to him there. Laban and his relatives also camped nearby.
26 Then Laban said to Jacob, "What have you done? You have tricked me. You have carried off my daughters like prisoners of war!
27 Why did you run away in secret? Why did you trick me? You did not even tell me. I would have sent you off with joy and singing, with drums and harps.
28 You did not even let me kiss my grandchildren and my daughters goodbye. You have done a foolish thing.
29 I have the power to do you great harm. But last night the God of your father spoke to me. He said, 'Be careful. Do not say anything to Jacob, either good or bad.'
30 You have gone away because you miss your father's house. But why have you stolen my gods?"
31 Jacob answered, "I was afraid. I thought you would take your daughters from me by force.

32 But if you find your gods with anyone here, that person will not live. In front of our relatives, look for yourself. See if anything here is yours, and take it back." Jacob did not know that Rachel had stolen the household gods.
33 So Laban went into Jacob's tent. Then he went into Leah's tent. Then he went into the tents of the two servant women. But he found nothing. Then he left Leah's tent and went into Rachel's tent.
34 Now Rachel had taken Laban's household gods. She had put them in the bag on her camel's saddle. She was sitting on them. Laban searched everything in the tent, but he found nothing.
35 Rachel said to her father, "Sir, do not be angry. I cannot stand up in your presence. I am having my period." So Laban searched, but he could not find the household gods.
36 Then Jacob became very angry. He challenged Laban. "What is my crime?" he said. "What sin have I done that you chase me down like this?
37 You have searched all my things. Have you found anything that belongs to you? Put it here in front of my relatives and yours. Let them judge between the two of us.
38 I have been with you for twenty years. Your sheep and goats have not lost their young. I have not eaten the rams from your flock.
39 I did not bring you any animal killed by wild beasts. I paid for the loss myself. You also demanded payment for what was stolen by day or night.
40 The heat burned me by day, and the cold froze me by night. I could not sleep.
41 For twenty years I have served in your household. Fourteen years were for your two daughters. Six years were for your flocks. And you changed my wages ten times!

42 If the God of my father, the God of Abraham and the Fear of Isaac, had not been with me, you would surely have sent me away empty-handed. But God has seen my hardship and the work of my hands. Last night he judged you."

43 Laban answered Jacob, "These daughters are my daughters. These sons are my sons. These flocks are my flocks. Everything you see is mine. Yet what can I do today about my daughters or the children they have borne?
44 Come now, let us make a covenant, you and I. Let it serve as a witness between you and me."
45 So Jacob took a stone and set it up as a pillar.
46 He said to his relatives, "Gather some stones." So they took stones and made a heap. There by the heap they ate together.
47 Laban called it Jegar-sahadutha. Jacob called it Galeed.
48 Laban said, "This heap is a witness between you and me this day." So the place was called Galeed.
49 It was also called Mizpah, because Laban said, "May the LORD keep watch between you and me when we are away from each other.
50 If you mistreat my daughters or take other wives, no one may be with us. But remember that God is a witness between you and me."
51 Laban also said to Jacob, "Here is the heap. Here is the pillar I have set up between you and me.
52 This heap is a witness. This pillar is a witness. I will not go past this heap to harm you. You will not go past this heap and pillar to harm me.
53 May the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor, the God of their father, judge between us." So Jacob took an oath in the name of the Fear of his father Isaac.
54 Then Jacob offered a sacrifice on the mountain. He invited his relatives to share a meal. After they had eaten, they spent the night on the mountain.
55 Early the next morning, Laban got up. He kissed his grandchildren and daughters and blessed them. Then he left to return home.

title: Genesis 32
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 Jacob also went on his way. Then the angels of God met him.
2 When Jacob saw them, he said, "This is the camp of God!" So he named that place Mahanaim, which means "two camps."
3 Jacob sent messengers ahead of him to his brother Esau. Esau lived in the land of Seir, the country of Edom.
4 He told them, "Say this to my master Esau: 'Your servant Jacob says: I have been staying with Laban until now.
5 I have oxen, donkeys, flocks, menservants, and maidservants. I send this message to my master, so I may find favor with you.'"
6 When the messengers returned to Jacob, they said, "We went to your brother Esau. Now he is coming to meet you — and 400 men are with him."
7 Jacob was very afraid and worried. He divided his people into two camps. He also divided the flocks, herds, and camels.
8 He thought, "If Esau comes and attacks one camp, the other camp can escape."
9 Then Jacob prayed, "O God of my father Abraham, God of my father Isaac, O LORD, you told me, 'Go back to your country and your relatives, and I will make you do well.'
10 I am not worthy of all the kindness and faithfulness you have shown your servant. I came across the Jordan with only my walking stick. But now I have become two camps.
11 Please save me from the hand of my brother Esau. I am afraid he will come and attack me, and the mothers and children with me.
12 But you have said, 'I will surely make you do well. I will make your descendants like the sand of the sea, too many to count.'"
13 Jacob spent the night there. From what he had with him, he chose a gift for his brother Esau:
14 200 female goats, 20 male goats, 200 female sheep, 20 male sheep,
15 30 mother camels with their young, 40 cows, 10 bulls, 20 female donkeys, and 10 male donkeys.
16 He gave them to his servants in separate herds. He told them, "Go ahead of me. Keep some distance between the herds."
17 He told the one in the lead, "My brother Esau will meet you. He will ask, 'Who is your master? Where are you going? Whose animals are these?'
18 Then you must say, 'They belong to your servant Jacob. They are a gift for my master Esau. And look, Jacob is right behind us.'"
19 He told the second, the third, and all who followed the herds the same thing. "Say this to Esau when you meet him.
20 Also say, 'Look, your servant Jacob is right behind us.'" Jacob thought, "I will calm Esau down with the gift before me. After that I can face him. Maybe he will accept me."
21 So Jacob's gifts went on ahead of him. He spent the night in the camp.
22 During the night, Jacob got up. He took his two wives, his two maidservants, and his eleven sons. They crossed the shallow place of the Jabbok.
23 He sent them across the stream with all his belongings.
24 So Jacob was left all alone. And there a man wrestled with him until daybreak.
25 The man saw that he could not win against Jacob. So he touched the socket of Jacob's hip. Jacob's hip was pulled out of place as they wrestled.
26 Then the man said, "Let me go, for it is daybreak." But Jacob replied, "I will not let you go until you bless me."
27 "What is your name?" the man asked. "Jacob," he replied.
28 Then the man said, "Your name will no longer be Jacob, but Israel. You have struggled with God and with men, and you have won."
29 Jacob asked, "Please tell me your name." But he replied, "Why do you ask my name?" Then he blessed Jacob there.
30 So Jacob named the place Peniel, which means "face of God." He said, "I have seen God face to face, and my life was saved."
31 The sun rose above him as he passed by Peniel. He was walking with a limp because of his hip.
32 So to this day the Israelites do not eat the muscle at the joint of the hip. This is because the socket of Jacob's hip was struck near that muscle.

title: Genesis 33
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 Jacob looked up and saw Esau coming toward him. Esau had 400 men with him. So Jacob divided the children among Leah, Rachel, and the two servant women.
2 He put the servant women and their children in front. Leah and her children came next. Rachel and Joseph were at the back.
3 But Jacob himself went on ahead. He bowed to the ground seven times as he came near his brother.
4 But Esau ran to meet him and hugged him. He threw his arms around his neck and kissed him. And they both wept.
5 Then Esau looked up and saw the women and children. He asked, "Who are these with you?" Jacob answered, "These are the children God has kindly given your servant."
6 Then the servant women and their children came near and bowed down.
7 Leah and her children also came near and bowed down. Then Joseph and Rachel came near and bowed down.
8 Esau asked, "What do you mean by sending all these flocks I met?" Jacob answered, "To find favor in your eyes, my lord."
9 Esau replied, "I already have plenty, my brother. Keep what you have for yourself."

10 But Jacob said, "No, please! If I have found favor in your eyes, then accept this gift from my hand. For to see your face is like seeing the face of God, now that you have welcomed me with kindness.
11 Please accept my gift. God has been kind to me, and I have all I need." So Jacob kept asking him until he accepted.
12 Then Esau said, "Let us be on our way. I will go ahead of you."

13 But Jacob replied, "My lord knows that the children are weak. I must also care for sheep and cattle that are feeding their young. If they are pushed too hard for even one day, all the animals will die.
14 Please let my lord go ahead of his servant. I will come on slowly, at a gentle pace for the animals and children. I will come to my lord at Seir."
15 Esau said, "Let me leave some of my people with you." But Jacob replied, "Why do that? Let me find favor in the eyes of my lord."
16 So that day Esau started on his way back to Seir.
17 But Jacob went on to Succoth. There he built a house for himself and shelters for his farm animals. That is why the place was called Succoth ("shelters").
18 Jacob had come from Paddan-aram. He arrived safely at the city of Shechem in the land of Canaan. He camped just outside the city.
19 He bought the plot of ground where he set up his tent. He bought it from the sons of Hamor, Shechem's father, for 100 pieces of silver.
20 There he set up an altar. He called it El-Elohe-Israel ("God, the God of Israel").

title: Genesis 34
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 Dinah was the daughter of Leah and Jacob. One day, she went out to visit the women of the land.
2 Shechem son of Hamor the Hivite was the prince of that region. He saw Dinah. He took her and forced himself on her. He violated her.
3 After this, his heart was drawn to Dinah, the daughter of Jacob. He loved the young girl. He spoke kindly to her.
4 So Shechem said to his father Hamor, "Get me this girl as a wife."
5 Jacob heard that Shechem had shamed his daughter Dinah. But his sons were out in the field with the animals. So Jacob said nothing until they came home.
6 Meanwhile, Shechem's father Hamor came to speak with Jacob.

7 When Jacob's sons heard what had happened, they came back from the field. They were full of grief and anger. Shechem had done a terrible wrong in Israel. He had lain with Jacob's daughter. This was a thing that must never be done.
8 But Hamor said to them, "My son Shechem longs for your daughter. Please give her to him as his wife.
9 Let us marry with each other. Give us your daughters. Take our daughters for yourselves.
10 You may live among us. The land will be open to you. Live here, move about freely, and own your own property."
11 Then Shechem said to Dinah's father and brothers, "Please grant me this favor. I will give you anything you ask.
12 Ask for a high bride-price and an expensive gift. I will give you whatever you ask. Only give me the girl as my wife!"
13 Shechem had dirtied their sister Dinah. So Jacob's sons answered him and his father Hamor with deceit.
14 They said, "We cannot do such a thing. To give our sister to an uncircumcised man would be a shame to us.
15 We will agree on one condition. Every one of your men must become circumcised, like us.
16 Then we will give you our daughters and take your daughters for ourselves. We will live among you and become one people.
17 But if you will not agree to be circumcised, we will take our sister and go."
18 Their offer seemed good to Hamor and his son Shechem.
19 The young man did not wait to do what they asked. He was delighted with Jacob's daughter. He was also the most respected in his father's house.
20 So Hamor and his son Shechem went to the gate of their city. They spoke to the men of their city.
21 "These men are at peace with us. Let them live and trade in our land. The land is large enough for them. Let us take their daughters in marriage. Let us give our daughters to them.
22 But the men will agree to live with us and be one people on only one condition. All our men must be circumcised, like them.
23 Will not their animals, their property, and all they own become ours? Let us agree to their terms, and they will live among us."
24 All the men who went out of the city gate listened to Hamor and his son Shechem. Every man in the city was circumcised.

25 Three days later, all the men were still in pain. Then two of Jacob's sons, Simeon and Levi, took their swords. They were Dinah's brothers. They went into the city, which was not expecting attack. They killed every man.
26 They killed Hamor and his son Shechem with their swords. They took Dinah out of Shechem's house. Then they went away.
27 Jacob's other sons came upon the killing. They took everything from the city, because their sister had been shamed.
28 They took the flocks, the herds, and the donkeys. They took everything in the city and in the field.
29 They carried off all the people's property. They took the women and children. They took everything from the houses.

30 Then Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, "You have brought trouble on me. You have made me a smell that the Canaanites and Perizzites hate. They are the people of this land. We are only a few. If they join together against me and attack me, I and my family will be destroyed."
31 But they replied, "Should our sister be treated like a prostitute?"

title: Genesis 35
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 Then God said to Jacob, "Get up. Go to Bethel and live there. Build an altar there to God. He showed himself to you when you ran from your brother Esau."
2 So Jacob said to his family and all who were with him, "Get rid of the foreign gods that are among you. Make yourselves clean and change your clothes.
3 Then let us get up and go to Bethel. I will build an altar there to God. He answered me when I was in trouble. He has been with me wherever I have gone."
4 So they gave Jacob all their foreign gods and all their earrings. Jacob buried them under the oak tree near Shechem.
5 As they set out, the terror of God fell on the towns around them. So no one chased Jacob's sons.
6 Jacob and everyone with him arrived in Luz (that is, Bethel) in the land of Canaan.
7 There Jacob built an altar. He called the place El-Bethel ("God of Bethel"). For God had shown himself to Jacob there when he ran from his brother.
8 Now Deborah, Rebekah's nurse, died. They buried her under the oak tree below Bethel. So Jacob named the place Allon-bacuth ("oak of weeping").
9 After Jacob came back from Paddan-aram, God appeared to him again and blessed him.
10 God said to him, "Your name is Jacob. But you will no longer be called Jacob. Your name will be Israel." So God named him Israel.
11 And God told him, "I am God Almighty. Have many children and grow in number. A nation — even a group of nations — will come from you. Kings will come from you.
12 The land I gave to Abraham and Isaac I will give to you. I will give this land to your children after you."
13 Then God went up from the place where he had spoken with him.
14 Jacob set up a pillar in that place — a stone marker. He poured out a drink offering on it. He also poured oil on it.
15 Jacob called the place where God had spoken with him Bethel.
16 Later, they set out from Bethel. While they were still some way from Ephrath, Rachel began to give birth. She had great pain.
17 During her hard labor, the birth helper said to her, "Do not be afraid. You are having another son."
18 As she was dying — for she was breathing her last — she named him Ben-Oni ("son of my sorrow"). But his father called him Benjamin ("son of the right hand").
19 So Rachel died. They buried her on the way to Ephrath (that is, Bethlehem).
20 Jacob set up a pillar on her grave. It marks Rachel's tomb to this day.
21 Israel set out again. He put up his tent beyond the Tower of Eder.
22 While Israel was living in that region, Reuben went in and slept with Bilhah, his father's second wife. And Israel heard about it. Jacob had twelve sons:
23 The sons of Leah were Reuben the firstborn of Jacob, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun.
24 The sons of Rachel were Joseph and Benjamin.
25 The sons of Rachel's servant Bilhah were Dan and Naphtali.
26 And the sons of Leah's servant Zilpah were Gad and Asher. These are the sons of Jacob. They were born to him in Paddan-aram.
27 Jacob returned to his father Isaac at Mamre, near Kiriath-arba (that is, Hebron). This is where Abraham and Isaac had stayed.
28 And Isaac lived 180 years.
29 Then he took his last breath and died. He joined his people, old and after a full life. His sons Esau and Jacob buried him.

title: Genesis 36
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 This is the record of Esau (that is, Edom).
2 Esau took his wives from the daughters of Canaan: Adah daughter of Elon the Hittite, Oholibamah daughter of Anah, granddaughter of Zibeon the Hivite,
3 and Basemath daughter of Ishmael and sister of Nebaioth.
4 Adah gave birth to Eliphaz for Esau. Basemath gave birth to Reuel.
5 Oholibamah gave birth to Jeush, Jalam, and Korah. These were the sons of Esau, born to him in the land of Canaan.

6 Later, Esau took his wives, sons, daughters, and all the people of his household. He took his livestock, all his other animals, and all he had gained in Canaan. He moved to a land far away from his brother Jacob.
7 They had too many possessions to live together. The land could not support them because of their livestock.
8 So Esau (that is, Edom) settled in the area of Mount Seir.
9 This is the record of Esau, the father of the Edomites, in the area of Mount Seir.
10 These are the names of Esau's sons: Eliphaz son of Adah, Esau's wife, and Reuel son of Basemath, Esau's wife.
11 The sons of Eliphaz were Teman, Omar, Zepho, Gatam, and Kenaz.
12 Timna, a second wife of Esau's son Eliphaz, gave birth to Amalek. These are the grandsons of Adah, Esau's wife.
13 The sons of Reuel were Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah. These are the grandsons of Basemath, Esau's wife.
14 These are the sons of Oholibamah, Esau's wife — daughter of Anah, granddaughter of Zibeon. She bore to Esau: Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
15 These are the chiefs among the sons of Esau. The sons of Eliphaz, the firstborn of Esau: chief Teman, chief Omar, chief Zepho, chief Kenaz,
16 chief Korah, chief Gatam, and chief Amalek. They are the chiefs of Eliphaz in the land of Edom, and the grandsons of Adah.

17 These are the sons of Reuel, Esau's son: chief Nahath, chief Zerah, chief Shammah, and chief Mizzah. They are the chiefs descended from Reuel in the land of Edom, and the grandsons of Basemath, Esau's wife.
18 These are the sons of Oholibamah, Esau's wife: chief Jeush, chief Jalam, and chief Korah. They are the chiefs descended from Oholibamah daughter of Anah, Esau's wife.
19 All these were the sons of Esau (that is, Edom), and these were their chiefs.
20 These are the sons of Seir the Horite, who were living in the land: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah,
21 Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan. They are the chiefs of the Horites, the descendants of Seir in the land of Edom.
22 The sons of Lotan were Hori and Hemam. Timna was Lotan's sister.
23 These are the sons of Shobal: Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam.
24 These are the sons of Zibeon: Aiah and Anah. This is the Anah who found the hot springs in the desert while he was feeding the donkeys of his father Zibeon.
25 These are the children of Anah: Dishon and Oholibamah daughter of Anah.
26 These are the sons of Dishon: Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran.
27 These are the sons of Ezer: Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan.
28 These are the sons of Dishan: Uz and Aran.
29 These are the chiefs of the Horites: chief Lotan, chief Shobal, chief Zibeon, chief Anah,
30 chief Dishon, chief Ezer, and chief Dishan. They are the chiefs of the Horites, by their divisions in the land of Seir.
31 These are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom before any king reigned over the Israelites:
32 Bela son of Beor reigned in Edom. The name of his city was Dinhabah.
33 When Bela died, Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah reigned in his place.
34 When Jobab died, Husham from the land of the Temanites reigned in his place.
35 When Husham died, Hadad son of Bedad reigned in his place. He defeated Midian in the country of Moab. The name of his city was Avith.
36 When Hadad died, Samlah from Masrekah reigned in his place.
37 When Samlah died, Shaul from Rehoboth on the river reigned in his place.
38 When Shaul died, Baal-hanan son of Achbor reigned in his place.
39 When Baal-hanan son of Achbor died, Hadad reigned in his place. His city was named Pau. His wife was Mehetabel daughter of Matred, daughter of Me-zahab.
40 These are the names of the chiefs descended from Esau, by their clans and lands, by their names: chief Timna, chief Alvah, chief Jetheth,
41 chief Oholibamah, chief Elah, chief Pinon,
42 chief Kenaz, chief Teman, chief Mibzar,
43 chief Magdiel, and chief Iram. These were the chiefs of Edom, by their settlements in the land they possessed. Esau was the father of the Edomites.

title: Genesis 37
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 Jacob lived in the land of Canaan. This was the land where his father had also lived.

2 This is the story of Jacob's family. Joseph was seventeen years old. He took care of the sheep with his brothers. He worked with the sons of Bilhah and Zilpah, his father's wives. And Joseph brought their father a bad report about them.
3 Israel loved Joseph more than any of his other sons. Joseph was born to him when he was old. So he made him an ornate robe — a beautiful long robe.
4 Joseph's brothers saw that their father loved him more than any of them. They hated him. They could not speak a kind word to him.
5 Then Joseph had a dream. He told it to his brothers. And they hated him even more.
6 He said to them, "Listen to this dream I had.
7 We were tying bundles of grain in the field. Suddenly my bundle stood up straight. Your bundles gathered around mine and bowed down to it."
8 His brothers asked him, "Do you really want to be king over us? Will you really rule over us?" So they hated him even more because of his dream and his words.
9 Then Joseph had another dream. He told it to his brothers. "Listen," he said. "I had another dream. This time the sun, the moon, and eleven stars were bowing down to me."
10 He told his father and his brothers. But his father scolded him. He said, "What kind of dream is this? Will your mother and your brothers and I really come and bow down to the ground before you?"
11 His brothers were jealous of him. But his father kept the matter in his mind.
12 Some time later, Joseph's brothers went to take care of their father's sheep near Shechem.
13 Israel said to Joseph, "Your brothers are with the sheep at Shechem. Get ready. I am sending you to them." Joseph answered, "I am ready."
14 Then Israel told him, "Go now. See how your brothers and the sheep are doing. Then come back and tell me." So he sent him from the Valley of Hebron. And Joseph arrived in Shechem.
15 A man found Joseph walking around in the field. He asked him, "What are you looking for?"
16 Joseph replied, "I am looking for my brothers. Please tell me where they are taking care of the sheep."
17 The man answered, "They have moved on from here. I heard them say, 'Let's go to Dothan.'" So Joseph went after his brothers. He found them at Dothan.
18 His brothers saw him in the distance. Before he reached them, they planned to kill him.
19 They said to one another, "Here comes that dreamer!
20 Come now. Let us kill him. Let us throw him into one of the pits. We can say that a wild animal has eaten him. Then we will see what happens to his dreams!"
21 When Reuben heard this, he tried to rescue Joseph. "Let us not take his life," he said.
22 "Do not spill his blood. Throw him into this pit here in the wilderness. But do not lay a hand on him." Reuben said this so he could rescue Joseph later and bring him back to his father.
23 So when Joseph came to his brothers, they took off his robe — the ornate robe he was wearing.
24 They grabbed him and threw him into the pit. The pit was empty. There was no water in it.

25 Then they sat down to eat a meal. They looked up and saw a group of traders coming. They were Ishmaelites coming from Gilead. Their camels were carrying spices, healing oil, and myrrh. They were on their way down to Egypt.
26 Then Judah said to his brothers, "What will we gain if we kill our brother and cover up his blood?
27 Come, let us sell him to the Ishmaelites. Let us not lay a hand on him. After all, he is our brother, our own flesh and blood." And his brothers agreed.
28 So when the Midianite traders passed by, the brothers pulled Joseph out of the pit. They sold him for twenty pieces of silver to the Ishmaelites. The traders took Joseph to Egypt.
29 Reuben came back to the pit. He saw that Joseph was not there. So he tore his clothes.
30 He went back to his brothers and said, "The boy is gone! Where can I turn now?"
31 Then they took Joseph's robe. They killed a young goat and dipped the robe in its blood.
32 They sent the ornate robe to their father. They said, "We found this. Look at it carefully. Tell us if it is your son's robe or not."
33 Their father knew it at once. He said, "It is my son's robe! A wild animal has eaten him. Joseph has surely been torn to pieces!"
34 Then Jacob tore his clothes. He put on rough cloth of mourning around his waist. He mourned for his son for many days.
35 All his sons and daughters tried to comfort him. But he refused to be comforted. "No," he said. "I will go down to the grave still mourning for my son." So his father wept for him.
36 Meanwhile, the Midianites sold Joseph in Egypt. They sold him to Potiphar, one of Pharaoh's officers, the captain of the guard.

title: Genesis 38
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 About that time, Judah left his brothers. He went to stay near a man named Hirah, an Adullamite.
2 There Judah saw the daughter of a Canaanite man named Shua. He married her and slept with her.
3 She became pregnant and gave birth to a son. Judah named him Er.
4 She became pregnant again and had another son. She named him Onan.
5 Then she had a third son and named him Shelah. She gave birth to him at Chezib.
6 Judah got a wife for Er, his firstborn son. Her name was Tamar.
7 But Er, Judah's firstborn, was evil in the sight of the LORD. So the LORD put him to death.
8 Then Judah said to Onan, "Sleep with your brother's wife. Do your duty as her brother-in-law. Give your brother children through her."
9 But Onan knew the child would not be his own. So whenever he slept with his brother's wife, he spilled his semen on the ground. He did not want to give his brother children.
10 What he did was evil in the sight of the LORD. So the LORD put Onan to death also.

11 Then Judah said to his daughter-in-law Tamar, "Go back to your father's house. Live there as a widow until my son Shelah grows up." But Judah was afraid that Shelah might also die, like his brothers. So Tamar went to live in her father's house.

12 After a long time, Judah's wife — the daughter of Shua — died. When Judah had finished his time of grief, he went up to Timnah. His friend Hirah the Adullamite went with him. The men there were cutting wool from his sheep.
13 Someone told Tamar, "Your father-in-law is going up to Timnah to cut wool from his sheep."

14 So she took off her widow's clothes. She covered her face with a veil to hide who she was. Then she sat at the entrance to Enaim, on the road to Timnah. She did this because she saw that Shelah had grown up, but she had not been given to him as a wife.
15 When Judah saw her, he thought she was a prostitute, because she had covered her face.
16 He did not know she was his daughter-in-law. He went over to her and said, "Come now, let me sleep with you." She asked, "What will you give me if I sleep with you?"
17 "I will send you a young goat from my flock," Judah answered. But she replied, "Only if you leave me something as a pledge until you send it."
18 "What pledge should I give you?" he asked. She answered, "Your seal and its cord, and the staff in your hand." So he gave them to her. Then he slept with her, and she became pregnant by him.
19 Then Tamar got up and went away. She took off her veil and put on her widow's clothes again.
20 Later, Judah sent his friend Hirah the Adullamite with the young goat. Hirah was to bring back the pledge from the woman. But he could not find her.
21 He asked the men of that place, "Where is the shrine prostitute who was beside the road at Enaim?" "There has been no shrine prostitute here," they answered.
22 So Hirah returned to Judah and said, "I could not find her. The men of that place said, 'There has been no shrine prostitute here.'"
23 "Let her keep the items," Judah replied. "If we ask more, we will become a joke. After all, I did send her this young goat, but you could not find her."
24 About three months later, someone told Judah, "Your daughter-in-law Tamar has acted like a prostitute. Now she is pregnant." Judah said, "Bring her out! Have her burned to death!"

25 As she was being brought out, Tamar sent a message to her father-in-law. She said, "I am pregnant by the man who owns these things." She added, "Please look at these. See if you recognize whose seal and cord and staff these are."
26 Judah recognized the items. He said, "She is more righteous than I am, since I did not give her to my son Shelah." And he did not sleep with her again.
27 When the time came for Tamar to give birth, there were twins inside her.
28 As she was giving birth, one baby put out his hand. The woman who was helping with the birth took a bright red string and tied it around his wrist. She said, "This one came out first."
29 But he pulled his hand back, and his brother came out instead. She said, "You have broken out first!" So he was named Perez.
30 Then his brother came out with the bright red string around his wrist. He was named Zerah.

title: Genesis 39
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 Meanwhile, Joseph had been taken down to Egypt. An Egyptian named Potiphar bought him from the Ishmaelites. Potiphar was one of Pharaoh's officers and the head of his guards.
2 And the LORD was with Joseph, so he became a successful man. He served in the house of his Egyptian master.
3 His master saw that the LORD was with him. He saw that the LORD gave him success in all he did.
4 So Joseph found favor in his eyes and became his personal helper. Potiphar put him in charge of his house. He put everything he owned into Joseph's care.
5 From that time, the LORD blessed the house of the Egyptian because of Joseph. The blessing of the LORD was on everything Potiphar had, in the house and in the field.
6 So Potiphar left everything he owned in Joseph's care. He did not worry about anything except the food he ate. Now Joseph was strong and good-looking.
7 After some time, his master's wife looked at Joseph with desire. She said, "Sleep with me."
8 But he refused. He said to his master's wife, "Look, with me here, my master does not worry about anything in his house. He has put everything he owns into my care.
9 No one in this house is greater than I am. He has kept nothing back from me, except you, because you are his wife. So how could I do such a wicked thing and sin against God?"
10 Potiphar's wife spoke to Joseph day after day. But he refused to go to bed with her or even be near her.
11 One day, however, Joseph went into the house to do his work. Not a single household servant was inside.
12 She grabbed Joseph by his cloak. She said, "Sleep with me!" But he left his cloak in her hand. He escaped and ran outside.
13 She saw that he had left his cloak in her hand and had run out of the house.
14 So she called her household servants. "Look," she said, "this Hebrew has been brought to us to shame us. He came to me so he could sleep with me. But I screamed as loud as I could.
15 When he heard me scream for help, he left his cloak beside me and ran out of the house."
16 So Potiphar's wife kept Joseph's cloak beside her until his master came home.
17 Then she told him the same story. "The Hebrew slave you brought us came to me to shame me.
18 But when I screamed for help, he left his cloak beside me and ran out of the house."
19 His master heard the story his wife told him. She said, "This is what your slave did to me." And he became very angry.
20 So Joseph's master took him and threw him into the prison. This was the place where the king's prisoners were kept. While Joseph was there in the prison,
21 the LORD was with him. He showed him kindness and granted him favor in the eyes of the prison warden.
22 So the warden put all the prisoners under Joseph's care. Joseph was responsible for everything that was done in the prison.
23 The warden did not worry about anything under Joseph's care. This was because the LORD was with Joseph and gave him success in whatever he did.

title: Genesis 40
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 Some time later, two of the king's servants made the king of Egypt angry. They were his cupbearer and his baker.
2 Pharaoh was angry with his two officers, the chief cupbearer and the chief baker.
3 So he put them in prison in the house of the captain of the guard. It was the same prison where Joseph was.
4 The captain of the guard told Joseph to take care of them. So Joseph attended them. They were in prison for some time.
5 One night, both men had a dream. The cupbearer and the baker each had his own dream. Each dream had its own meaning.
6 In the morning, Joseph came to them. He saw that they were very upset.
7 So he asked Pharaoh's officers, "Why do you look so sad today?"
8 "We both had dreams," they said. "But there is no one to tell us the meaning." Then Joseph said, "Do not the meanings belong to God? Tell me your dreams."
9 So the chief cupbearer told Joseph his dream. "In my dream, there was a vine in front of me.
10 On the vine were three branches. As it grew, the buds opened. The flowers opened, and the bunches became ripe grapes.
11 Pharaoh's cup was in my hand. I took the grapes and squeezed them into his cup. Then I placed the cup in his hand."
12 Joseph said, "This is the meaning: The three branches are three days.
13 In three days, Pharaoh will lift up your head. He will give you back your old job. You will put Pharaoh's cup in his hand again, just as you used to do.
14 But when all goes well with you, please remember me. Show me kindness. Tell Pharaoh about me, and get me out of this prison.
15 I was taken away by force from the land of the Hebrews. Even here, I have done nothing wrong. I should not be in this prison."
16 The chief baker saw that the meaning was good. So he said to Joseph, "I had a dream too. There were three baskets of white bread on my head.
17 In the top basket was all kinds of baked food for Pharaoh. But the birds were eating it out of the basket on my head."
18 Joseph said, "This is the meaning: The three baskets are three days.
19 In three days, Pharaoh will lift up your head — and hang your body on a pole. Then the birds will eat the meat from your body."
20 On the third day, it was Pharaoh's birthday. He gave a big meal for all his officers. In front of them, he lifted up the heads of the chief cupbearer and the chief baker.
21 Pharaoh gave the chief cupbearer his old job back. So he placed the cup in Pharaoh's hand again.
22 But Pharaoh hanged the chief baker, just as Joseph had said.
23 The chief cupbearer, however, did not remember Joseph. He forgot all about him.

title: Genesis 41
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 Two full years had passed. Then Pharaoh had a dream. He was standing beside the Nile River.
2 Seven cows came up from the river. They were fat and healthy. They began to eat grass among the reeds.
3 After them, seven more cows came up from the Nile. These cows were weak and thin. They stood beside the fat cows on the riverbank.
4 Then the weak, thin cows ate up the seven fat, healthy cows. After this, Pharaoh woke up.
5 But he fell back asleep and had a second dream. Seven heads of grain came up on one stalk. They were full and ripe.
6 After them, seven more heads of grain sprouted. These heads were thin and burned by the east wind.
7 The thin heads of grain swallowed up the seven full, ripe ones. Then Pharaoh woke up. He realized it was a dream.
8 In the morning his mind was troubled. So he called for all the magicians and wise men of Egypt. Pharaoh told them his dreams. But no one could interpret them for him.
9 Then the chief cupbearer said to Pharaoh, "Today I remember my mistakes.
10 Pharaoh was once angry with his servants. You put me and the chief baker in the prison of the captain of the guard.
11 One night we both had dreams. Each dream had its own meaning.
12 A young Hebrew man was there with us. He was a servant of the captain of the guard. We told him our dreams. He explained each one to us.
13 And it happened just as he had said. I was given back my old job. The other man was hanged."
14 So Pharaoh sent for Joseph. They quickly brought him out of the prison. Joseph shaved and changed his clothes. Then he went in before Pharaoh.
15 Pharaoh said to Joseph, "I had a dream, and no one can interpret it. But I have heard it said of you that when you hear a dream, you can interpret it."
16 "I cannot do it," Joseph replied. "But God will give Pharaoh the answer he needs."
17 Then Pharaoh said to Joseph, "In my dream I was standing on the bank of the Nile.
18 Seven cows came up from the river. They were healthy and fat. They began to eat grass among the reeds.
19 After them, seven more cows came up. They were weak, ugly, and thin. I have never seen such ugly cows in all the land of Egypt!
20 Then the thin, ugly cows ate up the seven fat cows that came first.
21 But when they had eaten them, no one could tell. The thin cows looked just as ugly as before. Then I woke up.
22 In my dream I also saw seven heads of grain. They were full and ripe. They grew on one stalk.
23 After them, seven more heads of grain sprouted. They were dried up, thin, and burned by the east wind.
24 The thin heads of grain swallowed the seven full ones. I told this dream to the magicians. But no one could explain it to me."
25 Then Joseph said to Pharaoh, "The dreams of Pharaoh are one and the same. God has revealed to Pharaoh what He is about to do.
26 The seven good cows are seven years. The seven ripe heads of grain are seven years. The dreams have the same meaning.
27 The seven thin, ugly cows that came up after them are seven years. The seven worthless heads of grain burned by the east wind are also seven years. They are seven years of famine.
28 It is just as I said to Pharaoh. God has shown Pharaoh what He is about to do.
29 Look — seven years of great plenty are coming throughout the land of Egypt.
30 But seven years of famine will follow them. Then all the plenty in Egypt will be forgotten. The famine will ruin the land.
31 The plenty in the land will not be remembered. The famine that follows will be so terrible.
32 The dream was given to Pharaoh in two forms. So the matter has been firmly decided by God. And God will do it soon.
33 Now, Pharaoh should look for a wise and able man. He should set him over the land of Egypt.
34 Let Pharaoh take action and appoint officials over the land. They should take a fifth of the harvest during the seven years of plenty.
35 Let them collect all the extra food from these good years. They should store the grain in the cities under Pharaoh's authority.
36 This food will be a reserve for the land during the seven years of famine. Then the country will not die in the famine."
37 This plan pleased Pharaoh and all his officials.
38 So Pharaoh asked them, "Can we find anyone like this man? The Spirit of God lives in him."
39 Then Pharaoh said to Joseph, "Since God has made all this known to you, there is no one as wise and able as you.
40 You shall be in charge of my house. All my people are to obey your commands. Only the throne will make me greater than you."
41 Pharaoh also told Joseph, "I now place you over all the land of Egypt."
42 Then Pharaoh took the signet ring off his own finger. He put it on Joseph's finger. He clothed Joseph in fine linen clothes. He placed a gold chain around his neck.
43 He had Joseph ride in his second chariot. Men called out before him, "Make way! Bow down!" So he placed him over all the land of Egypt.
44 Pharaoh said to Joseph, "I am Pharaoh. But without your permission, no one in Egypt shall lift a hand or foot."
45 Pharaoh gave Joseph the name Zaphenath-paneah. He also gave him Asenath as his wife. She was the daughter of Potiphera, priest of On (Heliopolis). And Joseph took charge of all the land of Egypt.
46 Joseph was thirty years old when he entered the service of Pharaoh king of Egypt. Joseph left Pharaoh's presence and traveled throughout the land of Egypt.
47 During the seven years of plenty, the land produced huge harvests.
48 During those seven years, Joseph collected all the extra food in Egypt. He stored it in the cities. In every city he laid up the food from the fields around it.
49 Joseph stored up so much grain — like the sand of the sea. He stopped keeping track of it. It was beyond measure.
50 Before the years of famine arrived, two sons were born to Joseph. Their mother was Asenath, daughter of Potiphera, priest of On.
51 Joseph named the firstborn Manasseh. He said, "God has made me forget all my trouble and all my father's household."
52 He named the second son Ephraim. He said, "God has made me fruitful in the land of my suffering."
53 Then the seven years of plenty in Egypt came to an end.
54 The seven years of famine began, just as Joseph had said. There was famine in every country. But there was food throughout the land of Egypt.
55 When extreme hunger came over Egypt, the people cried out to Pharaoh for food. He told all the Egyptians, "Go to Joseph. Do whatever he tells you."
56 When the famine had spread over all the land, Joseph opened up all the storehouses. He sold grain to the Egyptians. The famine was severe in the land of Egypt.
57 And every nation came to Joseph in Egypt to buy grain. The famine was severe over all the earth.

title: Genesis 42
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 Jacob heard that there was grain in Egypt. He said to his sons, "Why do you just keep looking at each other?"
2 "Look," he said, "I have heard there is grain in Egypt. Go down there and buy some for us. Then we will live and not die."
3 So ten of Joseph's brothers went down to buy grain from Egypt.
4 But Jacob did not send Joseph's brother Benjamin with them. He said, "I am afraid that harm might come to him."
5 So the sons of Israel went with others to buy grain. The famine had also spread to the land of Canaan.
6 Now Joseph was the governor over the land. He was the one who sold grain to all the people. So when his brothers arrived, they bowed down before him with their faces to the ground.

7 As soon as Joseph saw his brothers, he recognized them. But he pretended to be a stranger and spoke in a hard voice to them. "Where have you come from?" he asked. "From the land of Canaan," they replied. "We are here to buy food."
8 Joseph recognized his brothers, but they did not recognize him.
9 Joseph remembered his dreams about them. He said, "You are spies! You have come to see where our land is weak."
10 "No, my lord," they replied. "Your servants have come to buy food.
11 We are all sons of one man. Your servants are honest men, not spies."
12 "No," he told them. "You have come to see where our land is weak."
13 But they answered, "Your servants are twelve brothers, the sons of one man in the land of Canaan. The youngest is now with our father, and one is no more."
14 Then Joseph said, "Just as I said, you are spies!
15 This is how you will be tested. As surely as Pharaoh lives, you will not leave this place unless your youngest brother comes here.

16 Send one of your number to get your brother. The rest of you will be kept in prison, so that your words may be tested to see if you are telling the truth. If you are not, then as surely as Pharaoh lives, you are spies!"
17 So Joseph kept them in prison for three days.
18 On the third day he said to them, "Do this and you will live, for I fear God:
19 If you are honest men, let one of your brothers stay here in prison. The rest of you go and take grain back to your starving families.
20 But you must bring your youngest brother to me. Then your words can be tested, and you will not die." And they agreed to this.

21 Then they said to one another, "Surely we are being punished because of our brother. We saw how distressed he was when he begged us for his life. But we would not listen. That is why this trouble has come on us."
22 Reuben answered, "Didn't I tell you not to sin against the boy? But you would not listen! Now we must answer for his blood."
23 They did not know that Joseph understood them. He had been speaking through a translator.
24 Joseph turned away from them and cried. Then he turned back and spoke to them again. He took Simeon from them and tied him up before their eyes.

25 Then Joseph gave orders. He told his servants to fill their bags with grain. He told them to put each man's silver back in his sack. He also told them to give the brothers food for the trip. So this was done.
26 Then the brothers loaded the grain on their donkeys and left.
27 At the place where they stayed for the night, one of them opened his sack. He wanted to get food for his donkey. He saw his silver in the mouth of the sack.

28 "My silver has been returned!" he said to his brothers. "Here it is in my sack." Their hearts sank. They shook with fear and turned to one another. They said, "What is this that God has done to us?"
29 When they reached their father Jacob in the land of Canaan, they told him everything that had happened to them.
30 "The man who is lord of the land spoke in a hard voice to us. He said we were spies on the country.
31 But we told him, 'We are honest men, not spies.
32 We are twelve brothers, sons of one father. One is no more, and the youngest is now with our father in the land of Canaan.'
33 Then the man who is lord of the land said to us, 'This is how I will know if you are honest. Leave one brother with me. Take food back to your starving families, and go.
34 But bring your youngest brother back to me. Then I will know you are not spies but honest men. Then I will give your brother back to you, and you can trade in the land.'"
35 As they began emptying their sacks, each man saw his bag of silver in his sack! When they and their father saw the bags of silver, they were very afraid.
36 Their father Jacob said to them, "You have deprived me of my children. Joseph is no more, and Simeon is no more. Now you want to take Benjamin. Everything is against me!"
37 Then Reuben said to his father, "You may put both of my sons to death if I do not bring him back to you. Put him in my care, and I will bring him back."

38 But Jacob replied, "My son will not go down there with you. His brother is dead, and he alone is left. If harm comes to him on the journey, you will bring my gray head down to the grave in sorrow."

title: Genesis 43
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Genesis
paragraphs: 1 The famine in the land was still very bad.
2 Jacob's sons had eaten all the grain they brought from Egypt. So their father said, "Go back and buy us a little more food."
3 But Judah replied, "The man warned us with strong words. He said, 'You will not see my face again unless your brother is with you.'
4 If you will send our brother with us, we will go down and buy food for you.
5 But if you will not send him, we will not go. The man told us, 'You will not see my face again unless your brother is with you.'"
6 "Why did you bring this trouble on me?" Israel asked. "Why did you tell the man you had another brother?"

7 They replied, "The man asked us many questions about ourselves and our family. He asked, 'Is your father still alive? Do you have another brother?' We just answered his questions. How could we know he would say, 'Bring your brother here'?"
8 Then Judah said to his father Israel, "Send the boy with me. We will go at once. Then we may live and not die — we, you, and our children.
9 I will guarantee his safety. You may hold me personally responsible. If I do not bring him back and set him before you, I will bear the blame before you all my life.
10 If we had not delayed, we could have gone and come back twice by now."

11 Then their father Israel said to them, "If it must be so, then do this. Put some of the best things of the land in your bags. Carry them down as a gift for the man — a little balm and a little honey, spices and myrrh, pistachio nuts and almonds.
12 Take double the silver with you. Then you can return the silver that was put back in your sacks. Perhaps it was a mistake.
13 Take your brother as well. Return to the man at once.
14 May God Almighty grant you mercy before the man. Then he may let your other brother come back with Benjamin. As for me, if I lose my children, I lose them."
15 So the men took these gifts. They took double the silver, and Benjamin too. They hurried down to Egypt and stood before Joseph.

16 When Joseph saw Benjamin with his brothers, he spoke to the servant in charge of his house. He said, "Take these men to my house. Kill an animal for food and prepare it. They will eat with me at noon."
17 The man did as Joseph commanded. He took the brothers to Joseph's house.

18 But the brothers were afraid. They had been brought to Joseph's house. They said, "We were brought here because of the silver that was put back in our bags the first time. He wants to attack us and take us as slaves. He will also take our donkeys."
19 So they came near Joseph's servant. They spoke to him at the door of the house.
20 "Please, sir," they said, "we really did come down here the first time to buy food.
21 But when we stopped for the night, we opened our sacks. Look — each of us found his silver in the mouth of his sack! It was the full amount. We have brought it back with us.
22 We have brought more silver with us to buy food. We do not know who put our silver in our sacks."
23 "It is fine," said the servant. "Do not be afraid. Your God, the God of your father, has given you treasure in your sacks. I received your silver." Then he brought Simeon out to them.
